Tafseer:
The revelation of the Qur'an occurred under Allah's plan:
In the previous Surah, verses 3-5, reference was made to the great favor Allah Almighty bestowed for human instruction by revealing the Qur'an. Now, in this Surah, referencing it directly without any introduction, He states: 'Indeed, We revealed it in the Night of Decree' (inna anzalnahu fi laylatil-qadr). Although the objective pronoun 'it' (hu) appears without a prior explicit noun, the context is entirely clear; hence, this style of pronoun use is accepted. Furthermore, it is a proof that the Surahs in the Qur'an do not follow one another without any connection; rather, there is a deep outer and inner relationship between the preceding and succeeding Surahs.
The emphasis and authority in 'Indeed, We' (Inna) show that this Book is neither the result of the Prophet's own thought, nor does it contain any satanic whisper as the opponents assume; rather, Allah Almighty revealed it specifically from His side for the instruction and guidance of creation, without any other power having any share in it.
Laylat-ul-Qadr: 'Laylat-ul-Qadr' (the Night of Decree) refers to the night of the determination or division of matters, described in Surah Ad-Dukhan in these words:
(Indeed, We revealed it on a blessed night. Indeed, We were ever warning. On that night is made distinct every precise matter—as a command from Us. Indeed, We were ever sending [messengers].) (Ad-Dukhan, 44:3-5).
Pondering over this verse, two realities appear clearly:
First, Allah Almighty has appointed a specific blessed night in which all matters destined to be executed in this world are handed over to the angels who execute them.
Second, the Prophet's mission, the revelation of the Qur'an, and the warning to the Quraysh are among the grand matters whose execution was handed over to the respective angels in this blessed night—proving that this is not an ordinary event, but is among the grand plans and great designs of Allah Almighty, which is bound to reach its final completion.
Revealing the Qur'an in this night does not require that the entire Qur'an was revealed in a single night; rather, it is sufficient that the decision of its revelation was finalized, the task was handed over to Gabriel, and the first revelation descended in this night. Its progressive revelation over twenty-three years does not contradict this verse.

The grandeur of the Night of Decree: This explains the grandeur and blessing of the Night of Decree—showing that it is such a grand and blessed night that its true grandeur and blessing cannot be fully estimated. This grandeur is because grand decisions regarding this universe are made in it. When the days when worldly governments plan their annual projects are considered highly important, who can estimate the importance of the night in which the divine program for the entire universe is finalized and the decisions of the entire world are made!
Although these decisions relate to both mercy and punishment, selection and dismissal, victory and defeat, since they are from Him whose every decision is based on justice, mercy, and wisdom, and whose every act is for the welfare of this world, everything that occurs in this night is blessed as a whole. Thus, in the aforementioned verse of Surah Ad-Dukhan, He described it as 'a blessed night' (laylatin-mubarakah), and in this Surah, He declared it better than a thousand months. Describing these attributes is intended to warn the opponents of the Qur'an: if anyone regards this Book revealed in such a grand and blessed night as soothsaying, astrology, or poetry, he is unable to distinguish between precious gems and worthless stones. All paths of satanic whispers are closed in this blessed night. In it, the rain of revelation pours down, every drop of which is a precious gem.

The blessing of the Night of Decree: This describes the blessing of this night—showing that this single night is better than a thousand months. This 'better' is in terms of achieving objectives. Just as seasons, times, and periods are of value in this material world, they are of value in the spiritual world as well. Just as there are specific seasons and months for sowing specific crops, and if you sow in them, they grow and bear fruit, but if you ignore them, the long duration of other months cannot replace them, similarly, specific seasons, days, and times are appointed for specific tasks in the spiritual world. If those tasks are performed in these times, they yield the desired results; otherwise, the long duration of other times cannot replace them. For example, there is a specific day for Friday, a specific month for fasting, specific months and days for Hajj, and a specific day for the standing of Arafah. Allah Almighty has appointed great acts of worship with these specific days and times, whose reward is limitless; but their blessings appear in their true form only when they are observed with strict adherence to these times. Otherwise, the blessing is lost.
The same is the case with the Night of Decree. It is a night of great blessings and mercies. If a servant succeeds in seeking it, he can cover more stages of proximity to God in this single night than he can cover in a thousand months. 'A thousand months' can be used to express abundance, and also a specific proportion, but the objective remains the same—namely, great treasuries of spiritual life are hidden within the veils of this night. Fortunate are those who strive to seek it and succeed in finding it!
The difference regarding determining the Night of Decree: While it is agreed that the revelation of the Qur'an began in this night, and that it is a night of Ramadan, as stated elsewhere:
(The month of Ramadhan [is that] in which was revealed the Qur'an) (Al-Baqarah, 2:185),
the exact date of Ramadan is difficult to determine because of the different reports; the most that can be said is that it is most likely to be in the final ten nights of Ramadan.
Because of the different reports, some have raised the question: Is it specific to the month of Ramadan, or can it occur in other months as well? Similarly, is it fixed for one year or can it vary? Answering these questions requires a detailed investigation of all reports, which cannot be accommodated here. We have pointed to them solely for the benefit of scholars. If Allah Almighty grants us the opportunity to write our projected book on Hadith, these questions will be discussed there.

The primary task of this night: This explains its status as the night of the determination or division of matters. It is stated: in it, the angels and Gabriel descend with all the matters destined to be executed in the earth by His permission. This is expressed in Surah Ad-Dukhan as:
(On that night is made distinct every precise matter—as a command from Us) (Ad-Dukhan, 44:4-5).
That is: the precise matters He has determined in His wisdom are divided in this night, and the respective angels descend with His permission (SANCTION) to execute them. 'The Spirit' (Ar-Rooh) in this verse, context indicates, refers to Gabriel. Since he is the leader of all angels, he is mentioned specifically.

The devils are bound in this night: This explains the aspect described as: 'better than a thousand months'. It is stated: this night is peace in its entirety, and its blessing continues until the dawn.
'Salam' (peace) is in my view the predicate (khabar) of an omitted subject—namely: 'It is peace' (hiya salam). To focus the entire attention on the predicate, the subject is omitted. Just as 'Zayd is justice' (Zaydun 'adl) expresses intensity, 'salam' expresses intensity here, and I have preserved this in the translation.
While 'salam' guarantees safety from all calamities, it appears that the movement of all devils is restricted in this blessed night. Just as Allah protected the revelation by blocking every path of their access to the high assembly, similarly, the devils are under a heavenly curfew in the Night of Decree, which continues until the dawn. Consequently, they can neither move to learn the secrets of this night, nor interfere with its blessings.
